idea.hook: The Solar System's MOST volcanically active body?
facts: It's not Earth or Mars, but Jupiter's moon, Io!
Io is covered in over 400 active volcanoes, erupting sulfur plumes.
These plumes can blast 500 km (300 miles) high, constantly resurfacing the moon.
Its extreme activity is fueled by immense tidal forces from Jupiter, continuously heating Io's interior.
idea.payoff: This makes Io the most geologically active world in our Solar System, a fiery moon perpetually reborn!
idea.title: Io's Fiery Secret! 🔥🌋

scenes.text: The Solar System's MOST volcanically active body?
scenes.visual: A stylized question mark emerges from a field of stars, slowly rotating.

scenes.text: Not Earth or Mars... it's Jupiter's moon, Io!
scenes.visual: Images of Earth and Mars quickly fade out as a close-up of Io, a yellow-orange moon with visible volcanic vents, zooms into view.

scenes.text: Over 400 active volcanoes. Plumes blast 500 km high!
scenes.visual: A rotating 3D model of Io showing multiple active volcanic plumes erupting from its surface. A measurement line indicates 500km height next to a plume.

scenes.text: Fueled by Jupiter's immense tidal forces, continuously heating Io's interior.
scenes.visual: An animation showing Io orbiting Jupiter, with subtle pulsating energy lines radiating from Jupiter towards Io, illustrating tidal forces and internal heating.

scenes.text: Io: The MOST geologically active world! A fiery moon, perpetually reborn.
scenes.visual: A dramatic, high-resolution shot of Io with a massive, brightly glowing volcanic eruption dominating the foreground. The surface around it glows with heat.
